query: confirmatory test for HIV diagnosis in pregnant women

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Testing -- Diagnostic Tests -- Screening Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Of note, 4th generation assays can produce a false positive Ag/Ab, which is known to be false by the lack of both HIV 1/2Ab and a confirmatory RNA test. If the first step, Ag/Ab is positive, the test must be completed in its entirety with an HIV 1/2Ab and RNA test that results in a positive or negative final result. In the scenario of only partial results from a 4th-generation test (due to an insufficient sample), the results are not valid. See Figure 1.[CDC Stacks. 2018 Quick reference guide: Recommended laboratory HIV testing algorithm for serum or plasma specimens ]. Point-of-care testing for HIV continues to evolve in clinical practice. Currently, there is one single-use, rapid point-of-care assay capable of detecting both HIV-1 p24 antigen and antibodies to HIV-1/2. This test, performed with a fingerstick specimen, is CLIA-waived and identifies HIV infection earlier than antibody-only rapid tests. However, its sensitivity during early infection remains lower than that of the recommended laboratory-based, fourth-generation serum HIV screen. While several rapid antibody-only point-of-care tests are available, they are not routinely recommended in clinical care. Nonetheless, these tests may be valuable in specific circumstances, such as occupational exposures or when women present in labor without documented prenatal HIV screening. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV in Pregnancy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The Panel of Antiretroviral Guidelines for Adults and Adolescents in the United States suggests doing CBC, BUN, creatinine, and liver function tests before starting ART. These should then be repeated every three to six months. Additionally, urinalysis is recommended before starting a tenofovir-containing ART regimen and then every six months. HLA-B*5701 testing This should be done if abacavir-containing ART regimen is to be initiated Gestational diabetes screening

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Testing -- Introduction -- Who to Screen
retrieved_docs.content: Patients initiating preexposure prophylaxis (PrEP) should undergo HIV testing within 7 days of starting therapy, and again every 2 to 6 months while receiving this treatment. While on PrEP, HIV testing methods and frequency vary depending on the route and medication being used for PrEP. See below for further discussion. Pregnant women should be screened for HIV early in pregnancy. [7] Early detection facilitates timely interventions to reduce the risk of perinatal transmission.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV in Pregnancy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In the United States, the Panel on Treatment of HIV-infected Pregnant Women and Prevention of Perinatal Transmission recommends doing drug resistance testing before ART initiation and modification in all cases where the HIV RNA levels are >500 to 1000 copies/mL which is the threshold for drug resistance testing.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV in Pregnancy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Testing for tuberculosis is recommended for all patients with diagnosed HIV infection. [9] It can be safely done in HIV infected pregnant women if it has not been previously performed or if HIV is diagnosed for the first time during pregnancy. This is especially important because latent tuberculosis is more likely to transition into active infection in individuals who are HIV positive. [10] STI screening

retrieved_docs.title: HIV in Pregnancy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: For HIV positive pregnant women on ART regimens containing protease inhibitors, GDM screening should be performed earlier than the usual recommended age of 24-28 weeks because protease inhibitors tend to increase glucose intolerance in HIV-infected individuals. [7] Viral hepatitis testing

retrieved_docs.title: HIV in Pregnancy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If a woman presents in labor with unknown HIV status, offer her an opt-out screening option for HIV. The initial test is a combination immunoassay for HIV-1 and HIV-2 and an HIV RNA assay. If this initial test is positive, immediately start IV ZDV infusion. Women with positive initial tests are tested with antibody immunoassay, which differentiates HIV-1 and HIV-2 antibodies. Postpartum Management

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Relevant State Laws in the United States
retrieved_docs.content: Preliminary (unconfirmed) tests that yield positive results can be revealed only when decisions about medical management cannot await confirmation, including the need to provide recommendations to the person tested. Results can be revealed only to specific people: The person tested and clinicians responsible for the medical care and decision-making for the tested individual. The person tested and clinicians responsible for the care and decision-making of a newborn who may be affected by these results. Healthcare personnel subject to significant exposure from the person whose results were positive. Individuals tested using rapid testing technologies under manufacturers' instructions approved by the United States Food and Drug Administration.

retrieved_docs.title: Sexually Transmitted Infections -- Evaluation -- HIV
retrieved_docs.content: PCR testing is essentially diagnostic of an HIV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Testing -- Diagnostic Tests -- Screening Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: In cases of suspected acute HIV infection occurring fewer than 14 days after exposure, an HIV RNA test should be performed as the initial diagnostic test. Depending on the sensitivity of the RNA assay, viral RNA may be detectable as early as 5 to 10 days posttransmission. HIV RNA testing is also warranted when 4th-generation test results are indeterminate.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV in Pregnancy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In cases where a pregnant HIV positive woman has never received ART, especially if she is presenting late in her pregnancy, ART should be started before the drug resistance testing results are available and can be later modified accordingly. ART toxicity monitoring

retrieved_docs.title: HIV in Pregnancy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Unlike in HIV-negative women, serological testing for Toxoplasma gondii is recommended in HIV infected pregnant women. If T. gondii IgG is positive and CD4 count below 100/mL, then suppressive therapy is started.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Testing -- Diagnostic Tests -- Screening Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: If the antigen is positive while the antibody remains negative, a reflex HIV RNA test is required to confirm or exclude infection. A confirmatory test is performed to differentiate between HIV-1 and HIV-2 antibodies when both the antigen and the antibody are positive. This ability to distinguish HIV-1 from HIV-2 is a feature unique to 4th-generation testing and was not available in earlier test versions.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV in Pregnancy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Because of frequent coinfection, HIV positive pregnant women are screened for both HBV and HCV. In addition to the usual anti-HCV IgG and IgM, a patient with CD4 counts below 100/microL, and having a history of HCV risk factors should be given PCR testing for HCV RNA because serological testing for HCV can be falsely negative.

retrieved_docs.content: Following investigations are required to assess the infection and follow up with treatment: Gestational age estimation

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2117
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy encompass a range of maternal conditions that can affect pregnancy outcomes. Among these, قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (mild hyperemesis gravidarum) is identified as a specific child condition within the broader category of other pregnancy complications. Additionally, the category is classified to include الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before birth), highlighting the impact of substance use on maternal and fetal health. The classification also covers الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل (German measles during pregnancy), emphasizing the importance of infectious disease management in pregnant patients. Recognizing these interrelated conditions under the umbrella of other complications of pregnancy aids clinicians in comprehensive risk assessment, targeted prevention, and appropriate therapeutic interventions to improve maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: constitute major complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. Recognizing the placement of each specific condition within its broader category supports comprehensive assessment, risk stratification, and coordinated management of pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: serves as the central hierarchical category that integrates ante‑ and postpartum obstetric interventions—from diagnostic punctures and fetal monitoring to major surgical operations—within a framework that connects perinatal terminology, uterine physiology, female reproductive health, placental procedures and infection‑related outcomes, thereby guiding comprehensive maternal‑fetal assessment and patient‑centered care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1053
retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is a broad diagnostic category that includes COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY. Within this category, a specific manifestation is Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester, which is further classified as Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage. Recognizing that spotting in an unspecified trimester represents an early‑trimester hemorrhagic event and is considered a pregnancy‑related complication helps clinicians organize maternal conditions that affect pregnancy, delivery, or the puerperium and guides appropriate assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.content: disorders, and obstetric interventions together form a comprehensive framework for risk assessment, care planning, and management of maternal‑fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy encompass a range of maternal conditions, including قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (light vomiting during pregnancy) and فقر الدم في أثناء الحمل (anemia during pregnancy). This category is also classified as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before birth) and الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل (German measles during pregnancy), indicating that these specific complications are considered part of the broader group of other pregnancy‑related health issues. Recognizing these interrelated conditions aids clinicians in comprehensive prenatal assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: A poor reproductive history, reflecting previous obstetric complications or adverse outcomes, is clinically associated with an increased risk of preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This connection highlights the importance of detailed reproductive assessments in prenatal care to identify women who may be more likely to experience early delivery and to apply targeted monitoring and preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2913
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy is a category that includes several distinct conditions. It is classified as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before delivery). Within this category are the specific complications zwangerschap; vermoeidheid (pregnancy‑related fatigue) and طفح الحمل متعدد الأشكال (multiform rash of pregnancy). These entities represent various adverse events that can occur during gestation, underscoring the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and management of substance use, fatigue, and dermatologic manifestations to optimize maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy is a broad category that includes several specific maternal health issues. It has the child condition قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (light vomiting of pregnancy) and is classified as encompassing الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before delivery), الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل (German measles during pregnancy), and خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل (thyroid dysfunction in the gestational period). These various complications highlight the diverse risks that can affect pregnant individuals and underscore the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and appropriate management to protect both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 752
retrieved_docs.content: essential for clinicians to anticipate potential delivery complications, decide when normal delivery procedures are feasible, and implement appropriate interventions to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2089
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy represent a category of gestational health issues that includes قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة, a condition characterized by mild nausea or vomiting during pregnancy, and الهِربسُ الحَمْلِي, which refers to herpes infection occurring in the pregnant patient. This group of complications is classified as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة, indicating that drug dependence or substance use prior to delivery is considered a related factor within the spectrum of other pregnancy complications. Recognizing these specific conditions and their classification helps clinicians address a broader range of maternal health concerns and tailor preventive and therapeutic strategies accordingly.
